title: History’s hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita
subtitle: Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits’ in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]
caption:
History's hockey stick: Worldwide historical real gross domestic product per capita, 1000 to 2018

Unit 1 'The capitalist revolution: prosperity, inequality, and planetary limits' in The CORE Team, The Economy 2.0 Microeconomics. Available at: https://tinyco.re/19274920 [Figure 1.1]

The line chart traces the trajectory of real gross domestic product per capita for five major economies from the year 1000 to 2018, with values expressed in 2011 US dollars adjusted for Purchasing Power Parity. For the majority of the timeline, spanning from 1000 to roughly 1800, the lines for the United Kingdom, Italy, Japan, China, and India remain compressed near the horizontal axis, fluctuating within a narrow band between $0 and $5,000. The data shows a distinct divergence beginning in the 19th century, where the lines for the United Kingdom, Italy, and Japan begin a sustained upward climb. By 2018, the United Kingdom and Japan reach the highest points on the vertical scale, terminating in the $35,000 to $40,000 range. Italy follows a similar upward path, ending slightly lower in the $30,000 to $35,000 band. In contrast, the lines for China and India remain flat near the bottom of the chart until the mid-20th century. From approximately 1980 onward, the line for China exhibits a steep vertical ascent, rising from below $5,000 to roughly $15,000 by the final data point. The line for India shows a more gradual increase during the same recent period, ending below $10,000. The visual structure of the graph displays a long period of stagnation followed by a rapid, near-vertical expansion for all plotted nations in the modern era, creating a shape that resembles the blade of a hockey stick.
